fuzzy_prd:
We need a small, self-contained utility library for an e-book reading engine that handles three categories of low-level string operations. First, the library must be able to look at a resource reference and tell the caller whether that reference points inside the publication bundle or out to the internet. An empty string should be treated as an internal reference, and passing nothing at all should raise an error in the same manner as the authentication module does for missing tokens. Second, the library must be able to extract the directory portion from a file path, and also combine a base directory with a relative file reference while correctly resolving any parent-directory traversal segments; both operations must tolerate messy duplicate slashes by normalizing them away. Third, the library must split a hyperlink reference into its resource address and its optional in-page anchor, keeping track of whether an anchor was present at all (so a missing anchor can be distinguished from an empty one). Finally, the library must parse narration timing values used in synchronized audio playback. Timing values come in two flavors: one uses colon-separated fields for hours, minutes, and seconds, and the other uses a plain number followed by a metric unit suffix. Both flavors must be handled by a single parser that normalizes the result into hour, minute, second, and millisecond components and produces a canonical string rendering in the same format as the timestamp formatting used elsewhere in the narration subsystem. Any malformed input should cause the parser to signal failure rather than throw an exception.

One small follow-up from the questions that came back: for the local vs external check, the rule is intentionally very literal. A path is non-local if and only if it contains the substring '://' anywhere in the string. If it does not contain '://', it is local, including an empty string. If the path argument is null, it throws ArgumentNullException with param name 'path', and the dispatcher should surface that as error=argument_null / param=path. Same general pattern applies anywhere this utility is supposed to reject a missing required argument: Throws ArgumentNullException with the parameter name of the offending argument, which the dispatcher catches and renders as error=argument_null followed by param=<argument_name>. See ContentPathUtils.IsLocalPath and UrlParser constructor for the pattern.

Also, one edge case on Combine: when both inputs are null, or both resolve to null/empty, the function returns null internally. In that case the dispatcher does not make up a path string, it renders the null result as the literal token '<null>', so the output is exactly combined=<null>.

For narration times, the canonical output format is h:mm:ss[.fff]. That means the hour is unpadded, minute and second are always two digits, and the fractional part '.fff' is included only when the millisecond value is non-zero. The examples to line up with are 2:30:03, 0:00:10.500, and 0:00:00.005, including the cases called out as ms=500 and ms=5. On the unit-style inputs, the only valid suffixes are exactly 'h', 'min', 's', and 'ms'. They are case-sensitive and must be lowercase. 'H', 'Min', 'S', 'MS', 'Sec' and any other casing should fail. If there is no unit at all, treat the number as seconds. There is also a hard upper bound for timecount magnitude: (Int64)Int32.MaxValue * MILLISECONDS_IN_HOUR, which equals (long)2147483647 * 3600000 = 7730941132800000000 milliseconds. Anything larger than that should make TryParse return false rather than throw.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public static bool TryParse(string smilClock, out EpubNarrationTimestamp result)

trigger_keywords: what decimal separator is allowed in clock values
can I use a comma for fractional seconds
what happens if comma is used instead of dot
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only the dot '.' is accepted as the decimal separator in colon-notation clock values. A comma ',' used as a decimal separator causes the parser to reject the value and return valid=false. This is enforced through Decimal.TryParse with InvariantCulture.

trigger_keywords: what happens when whitespace separates number from unit in timecount
can there be a space before the unit
whitespace between number and metric unit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Whitespace between the numeric part and the metric unit is not allowed. If there is any whitespace between the number and the unit (e.g. '3 h'), the parser rejects the value and returns valid=false, because the non-digit/non-dot character detection occurs inline without trimming inner whitespace.

trigger_keywords: are minutes and seconds range-validated in clock notation
what range must minutes and seconds be in
out of range minute or second
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In colon-notation, minutes and seconds must each be in the range 0..59 inclusive. A value of 60 or greater for either field causes rejection (valid=false). The hour field has no upper bound and only must be non-negative (>=0).

trigger_keywords: how are directory traversal segments resolved in combine
what happens when traversal exhausts the directory
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each leading '../' in the file argument strips the last segment from the directory (up to the last '/'). If traversal exhausts the directory entirely (directory becomes empty or has no more slashes), the remaining file reference is returned as-is without a leading slash. No error is thrown for over-traversal.

trigger_keywords: how does the anchor_present flag work
distinguish missing anchor from empty anchor
what is output when href has # at end
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: anchor_present=true if and only if a '#' character was found in the href string; anchor_present=false otherwise. When '#' is present, the anchor text may be empty (e.g. 'file.html#' yields anchor_present=true, anchor=). When '#' is absent, anchor_present=false and anchor= (empty). A null href raises error=argument_null / param=url.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: in the same format as the timestamp formatting used elsewhere in the narration subsystem
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The h:mm:ss[.fff] format implemented in EpubNarrationTimestamp.ToString() at golden/Source/VersOne.Epub/Entities/EpubNarrationTimestamp.cs, using format string: $"{Hour}:{Minute:D2}:{Second:D2}{(Millisecond > 0 ? (\".\"+Millisecond.ToString(\"D3\")) : String.Empty)}"

oracle_data.fallback_response: This question does not match any specific documented constraint for this task. Please consult the source code under golden/Source/VersOne.Epub/Utils/ and golden/Source/VersOne.Epub/Readers/SmilClockParser.cs for authoritative implementation details.

feature1_1_local_path_classification.jso.description: Classify a content reference as either a local (in-package) path or a remote/absolute reference. The classifier inspects the raw reference string: a reference is treated as local when it does NOT contain a scheme separator (the '://' sequence), and as non-local otherwise. An empty reference is considered local. A null reference is rejected as a missing-argument error. The output echoes the inspected reference and reports the local/non-local verdict.

feature1_2_directory_path.json.description: Extract the directory portion of a content file path. The directory is everything that precedes the last forward-slash separator in the path; the segment after the final slash (the file name) is discarded. A path that contains no slash has an empty directory. A path that ends with a slash yields the part before that trailing slash. Parent-traversal segments ('..') that appear before the final slash are preserved verbatim. The output reports the resulting directory string (which may be empty).

feature1_3_combine_path.json.description: Combine a base directory with a relative file reference into a single normalized content path. Before combining, repeated consecutive slashes in either input are collapsed to a single slash. If the directory is null or empty, the file reference alone is the result; if the file reference is null or empty, the directory alone is the result; if both are null, the result is null. Each leading parent-traversal segment ('../') in the file reference removes the last segment of the directory and is itself consumed; traversal that exhausts the directory leaves the remaining file reference as the result. The directory and the (remaining) file reference are otherwise joined with a single slash. A null combined result is reported with the literal token <null>.

feature2_href_reference.json.description: Split a hyperlink reference into its path component and its optional fragment (anchor) component. The fragment is the part that follows the first '#' character; the path is everything before it. When the reference contains no '#', there is no fragment and the whole reference is the path. The output reports the path, whether a fragment was present, and the fragment text (empty when absent). A null reference is rejected as a missing-argument error.

feature3_1_clock_value.json.description: Parse a media-overlay clock value written in colon-separated notation into a normalized timestamp. The value may be a full clock of the form hours:minutes:seconds (with optional fractional seconds) or a partial clock of the form minutes:seconds (with optional fractional seconds); surrounding whitespace is ignored. Minutes and seconds must each be in the range 0..59; hours must be non-negative. Fractional seconds are truncated to whole milliseconds (digits beyond the third are discarded, not rounded). A blank value (null, empty, or whitespace-only), a value with too many colon-separated parts, an empty or non-numeric or negative or out-of-range component, or a non-dot decimal separator is rejected. On success the output reports the broken-down hour, minute, second, and millisecond components plus a canonical h:mm:ss[.fff] rendering (the fractional part is shown only when non-zero); on failure it reports that the value is not valid.

cases.expected_output:
valid=true
hour=2
minute=30
second=3
millisecond=0
timestamp=2:30:03

valid=true
hour=0
minute=0
second=10
millisecond=500
timestamp=0:00:10.500

feature3_2_timecount_value.json.description: Parse a media-overlay clock value written in timecount notation into a normalized timestamp. A timecount is a non-negative number, optionally fractional, followed by an optional metric unit: 'h' for hours, 'min' for minutes, 's' for seconds, or 'ms' for milliseconds; when no unit is given the number is interpreted as seconds. Surrounding whitespace is ignored. The numeric magnitude is converted to whole milliseconds (the fractional part beyond a millisecond is truncated, not rounded) and then broken down into hour, minute, second, and millisecond components, where the minute and second components wrap at 60 and the hour component absorbs the overflow. A value whose magnitude exceeds the maximum supported timecount, a negative number, an unrecognized or wrongly-cased unit, or a unit preceded by whitespace is rejected. On success the output reports the broken-down components plus a canonical h:mm:ss[.fff] rendering (the fractional part is shown only when non-zero); on failure it reports that the value is not valid.

cases.expected_output:
valid=true
hour=3
minute=12
second=0
millisecond=0
timestamp=3:12:00

valid=true
hour=0
minute=0
second=0
millisecond=5
timestamp=0:00:00.005
